region_of_origin: Indian subcontinent; widely adopted in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002700 covers biryani as a category; no chicken-biryani-specific term exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for chicken biryani exists in the clinical terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of chicken biryani is approximately 350 g, comprising a generous portion of spiced basmati rice with bone-in or boneless chicken pieces.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Biryani — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN 2017 reports chicken biryani as a composite dish. Values per 100 g edible portion used directly. This is the highest-priority approved source for Indian-origin dishes served in Singapore.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for chicken biryani was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 54 is estimated from basmati rice (GI 52–58, mean ~54 per Atkinson et al. 2008, Table A) as the dominant glycemic carbohydrate component (~55–60% of dish weight). Protein and fat from chicken and ghee further attenuate postprandial glucose response, supporting a low-GI classification. GL per serving = (54 × 19.2 × 3.50) / 100 = 36.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg/100 g; ~1120 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a standard serving and request reduced-salt preparation, as a full 350 g serving provides approximately 1120 mg sodium, representing nearly half the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (GL ~36) despite low GI of basmati rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the GI of chicken biryani is low (~54) due to basmati rice and protein-fat attenuation, the large carbohydrate load per standard serving (~67 g carbohydrate per 350 g plate) produces a high glycemic load; people with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict to a half-portion (~175 g) and pair with non-starchy vegetables to moderate postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee and chicken skin (~1.6 g/100 g; ~5.6 g per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A standard serving contributes approximately 5.6 g saturated fat, which is clinically relevant for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing skinless chicken and requesting ghee-reduced preparation can lower saturated fat intake and support LDL cholesterol management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Biryani is a fragrant, spiced rice dish of South Asian origin that has become a staple of Singapore's Indian-Muslim and Indian culinary heritage. It is prepared by layering or mixing partially cooked basmati rice with marinated chicken pieces — typically bone-in or boneless — that have been slow-cooked or dum-cooked with a blend of whole and ground spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, ginger, garlic, and chilli. Ghee, yogurt, caramelised onions, fresh mint, and saffron-infused milk are characteristic additions that impart a rich, aromatic flavour and a moist, slightly sticky texture. The dish is golden-yellow to orange in colour from turmeric and saffron. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 525 kcal, 30 g protein, 17 g fat, and 67 g carbohydrate. The basmati rice base gives a low-to-moderate glycemic index (~54), but the large carbohydrate portion results in a high glycemic load per serving (~36), making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate-to-high (~1120 mg per serving), relevant for hypertension management. Saturated fat from ghee and chicken skin (~5.6 g per serving) warrants attention for cardiovascular disease patients. Chicken biryani is a good source of protein, B vitamins (niacin, B6, B12), iron, and phosphorus. In Singapore, it is commonly served at Indian-Muslim restaurants, hawker centres, and festive occasions, often accompanied by raita (yogurt condiment), papadum, and a boiled egg.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken biryani?
Is chicken biryani good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from ICMR-NIN 2017, the highest-priority approved source for Indian-origin dishes, which reports chicken biryani as a composite dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice GI from Atkinson et al. 2008 (peer-reviewed), as no direct GI measurement for chicken biryani was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because the GI is estimated rather than directly measured.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN 2017 source for this dish. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN 2017 source for this dish. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN 2017 source for this dish. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in this preparation per ICMR-NIN data. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate from basmati rice GI data (Atkinson et al. 2008); no direct GI measurement for chicken biryani was located in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving calculated as (54 × 19.2 × 3.50) / 100 = 36.3. Input food_name contained menu-style clutter ('Rice - Biryani 米饭 -印度比尔亚尼菜'); canonical English name 'Chicken Biryani' used. Restaurant source: Maharani Table - Boat Quay, field survey data (price SGD 17.90). gi_category is 'low' as GI 54 ≤ 55 per Veritas threshold rules.
